title: Heritage scrapbook archival page
subtitle: Mounting family photographs and documents for long-term preservation
excerpt: An archival scrapbook page keeps family photographs, letters, and documents stable for generations by using acid-free materials throughout. This tutorial covers choosing the right adhesives and papers, how to mount photographs without damaging them, and how to add handwritten captions that won't migrate into the photographs over time.
sourceNotes: Cyndy Rymer and Nancy Kummerow, Memories of a Lifetime: Vintage Photographs (2006, Sterling), archival materials chapter. Acid-free standards follow the Library of Congress preservation guidance for personal papers (loc.gov/preservation).

glossaryTerms.definition: Materials with a pH of 7 or above (neutral to alkaline). Paper, card, and adhesives that are not acid-free release acids that brown and weaken adjacent paper over decades.

Mounting photographs by sliding their corners into small triangular sleeves that are glued to the page. The photograph itself is not adhesive-contacted, so it can be removed and repositioned without damage.

content.text: The key principle of heritage scrapbooking is reversibility: attach nothing permanently to original photographs or documents, and use only

materials throughout. An original photograph glued to an acidic page, or stored under a plastic sleeve that off-gasses, will be damaged within 20 years. Correct materials extend the stable lifespan to 100 years or more.

Never use: rubber cement (acidic, leaves a residue that stains), ordinary sellotape (yellows and loses adhesion after 10 years), PVC plastic sleeves (PVC off-gases and transfers to the photograph surface), or regular ballpoint ink for captions (acidic and bleeds in damp conditions). The simple alternatives are: photo corners for all photographs, acid-free PVA for paper elements, archival pen for captions, and polyester or polypropylene sleeves for loose items.
Position the photograph on the page. Apply one

to the page at each corner of the photograph, using the adhesive backing on the corner. Slide the photograph corners into the four sleeves. The photograph is held firmly but can be lifted out at any time without damage. For a more decorative look, use black photo corners on cream page backgrounds, a style from 1920s-1940s family albums.

For letters, certificates, and ephemera that you want to attach permanently, use a thin coat of acid-free PVA on the back of the document, applied to only 20% of the document area (the four corners and centre), not the full surface. This minimises the adhesive contact while holding the document flat. If the document has significant value, make a photocopy and mount the copy; keep the original separate.

Write captions on a separate small card and attach the card to the page rather than writing directly below the photograph. This allows you to correct or update captions without altering the page layout. Write names, dates, and places: the question future viewers most often have is simply who is in the photograph and when it was taken. Don't write on the back of original photographs, even a soft pencil indent damages the emulsion over decades.
